description: Testing how much explanatory structure is lost at each compression level

compression_a.description: All discovered categories from Q3 clustering

compression_b.description: Collapsed into four categories: Distinction / Comparison / Preservation / Persistence

mapping.Variation and Measurement: Preservation (testing whether patterns persist under variation)
mapping.Removal and Degradation Testing: Preservation (testing whether function persists under removal)

compression_b.information_lost: Moderate - five categories collapsed to four; some nuance lost

compression_c.description: Collapsed into two categories: Change occurred / No change occurred
compression_c.information_lost: High - all methodological nuance lost; only binary distinction remains
compression_c.note: All 20 studies involved change; no study had 'no change occurred'

compression_a_to_b.information_lost: One category lost (5 -> 4); some methodological nuance lost
compression_a_to_b.explanatory_structure_preserved: High - the four categories still capture the main types of change

compression_b_to_c.information_lost: Three categories lost (4 -> 2); all methodological nuance lost
compression_b_to_c.explanatory_structure_preserved: Low - only binary change/non-change remains; no information about what type of change

compression_a_to_c.information_lost: Four categories lost (5 -> 2); almost all methodological nuance lost
compression_a_to_c.explanatory_structure_preserved: Very low - only binary change/non-change remains

key_finding: Compression C loses almost all explanatory structure. The ontology (5 categories) carries real information beyond 'change occurred'. The question is whether the 5-category ontology is the right level, or whether a simpler ontology (like 4 categories) would suffice.
implication: The archive's explanatory structure is not overbuilt at the 5-category level, but may be overbuilt at finer granularity. The 4-category compression (Compression B) preserves most of the explanatory structure while being simpler.
